data.title: FARO Technologies: A Strategic Pivot That Fell Short
data.description:
Thrust of my analysis

On May 6, 2025, FARO Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:FARO) announced an agreement for AMETEK to acquire all the outstanding shares of Faro common stock for $ 44 per share in cash, representing an approximate 40% premium to Faro's closing price on May 5, 2025. Then, on 15 July, at the Special Meeting to consider the sale to AMETEK, 99% of the shareholders voted in favour of the sale.

The purpose of this article is to evaluate whether the $ 44 per share acquisition price represented fair value for shareholders and to explore why Faro was unable to independently unlock the potential of its pivot into spatial data solutions.

While the company was positioned in high-growth markets, years of losses, weak returns on capital, and poor capital allocation eroded its ability to self-fund a sustainable turnaround.

Faro’s story serves as a case study in the risks of business model pivots. The company faced numerous challenges, including executing operational transformations and maintaining capital discipline. It illustrates that even with favourable market trends, strategy alone is insufficient without operating leverage and consistent value creation.

A decade of evolution

Over the past decade, Faro transformed from a fragmented hardware supplier into an integrated spatial data solutions company. The 2020 global restructuring plan was the critical turning point. It streamlined operations, sharpened focus, and laid the structural groundwork for the company’s pivot to a recurring, platform-based business model.

Faro is today a niche technology company that develops and sells integrated 3D measurement hardware and software solutions used in capturing, analysing, and utilizing spatial data from the real world.

The company serves global customers across three primary end markets - manufacturing (e.g., automotive, aerospace), architecture, engineering & construction, and public safety analytics (e.g., crime scene reconstruction, fire investigations)

Unfortunately, the company did not provide any breakdown of its revenue by end markets. Rather it only broke down its revenue into products (accounting for about 3/4 of the past 2 years revenue) and services.

Faro serves the global market. In 2024, the US and Canada accounted for about 41% of the revenue. The balance came from its international operations.

Faro’s business today spans multiple converging growth markets - 3D scanning hardware, digital twin platforms (transforming real-world 3D scan data into virtual replicas), and scanning services.

The following market research report shows strong growth in each of these areas, validating Faro’s shift from a device maker to a data-driven platform company.

“The global 3D scanner market size accounted for USD 1.98 billion by 2024, and…projected …at a CAGR of 13.72% from 2025 to 2034.” Precedence Research

“The global digital twin market size was estimated at USD 24.97 billion in 2024 and is projected… grow at a CAGR of 34.2% from 2025 to 2030.” Grand View Research

“The 3D laser scanning services market size is expected to see strong growth in the next few years. It will grow to $12.55 billion in 2029 at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 9.0%.” The Business Research Company

Lacklustre performance

While its business pivot involved several acquisitions, Faro also had to undertake some divestitures and closures post-2019. As such, there was hardly any revenue growth over the past decade. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

More troubling was that the company incurred an operating loss from 2019 to 2023. In Chart 1, the gap between revenue and total cost represents the operating profits. We have an operating loss for the year if the revenue is less than the respective total cost.

The PAT position was more dire as the company had to contend with restructuring charges.

Given the loss picture, you should not be surprised that the past decade ROIC and ROE were negative.

Note to Op Profit Profile. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, R&D and Others.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

Better prospects ahead

Moving forward, it is obvious that Faro has to operate well above its breakeven level to generate value-creating returns (i.e., returns that are greater than the cost of capital). There are several options for the company.

i) Grow its revenue. I estimated that, based on the 2024 margin and costs, the breakeven revenue is $331 million.

ii) Expand its margin. You can see from the left part of Chart 1 that its gross profit margin has been declining since 2019, although there was a reversal in 2024. This picture is reflected in the contribution margin. Refer to the right part of Chart 1

iii) Reduce its fixed cost. You can see that the Selling, General and Administration (SGA) margin has been increasing since 2015 to peak in 2019. It declined thereafter as per the left part of Chart 1. The other positive sign is that the fixed cost margin in 2024, at 58% is lower than the 2019 peak of 61 %.

iv) Combinations of revenue growth, margin expansion, and fixed cost control.

Faro’s Q1 2025 results point to some progress across all three levers needed to move beyond breakeven and into value-creating territory.

i) While total sales were down 1.6% year over year, it long-term plans for acquisitions would boost revenue.

ii) The Q1 2025 gross profit margin of 57% was significantly better than the 51% for Q1 2024.

iii) In Q1 2025, the company incurred $43.3 million for SGA and R&D compared to $ 48.6 million in Q1 2024.

They indicate that the transformation efforts over the past five years are beginning to yield sustainable financial traction.

Peer comparison

I compared Faro’s performance with 4 publicly-listed peers stated in its Form 10k.

i) Hexagon AB (HEXA-B). A global leader in digital reality, Hexagon combines precision sensors, software, and analytics for industrial metrology, geospatial, autonomous, and manufacturing applications. Hexagon is Faro’s scale peer in 3D measurement, data platforms, and software-driven automation, providing a benchmark for end‑to‑end spatial digitization.

ii) Nikon Corporation (7731). Nikon’s metrology arm offers comprehensive industrial inspection systems - from laser scanners to X‑ray/CT systems and metrology software - for high‑precision manufacturing needs. It directly competes with Faro in industrial measurement and inspection.

iii) Topcon Corporation (TSE: 7732). A diversified Japanese firm delivering precision positioning, surveying, infrastructure, and healthcare solutions - leveraging optical hardware and connectivity for spatial intelligence. Topcon overlaps with Faro in positioning and reality capture tools, particularly in architecture, engineering & construction, and infrastructure markets, making it a relevant peer in construction tech dynamics.

iv) Trimble Inc. (TRMB). A Silicon Valley–based hardware–software pioneer that links total stations, 3D scanners, and cloud analytics to power construction, geospatial, agriculture, and transportation workflows. Trimble shares Faro’s mix of measurement hardware and vertically tailored software, enabling apples-to-apples comparisons on recurring revenue and workflow integration.

As shown in Table 1, Faro is the smallest in terms of 2024 revenue and one of the 2 with negative growth rates.

Overall, Faro had the worst performance among the peers. Refer to Charts 2 and 3. Given its standstill revenue and the many years of losses, you should not be surprised by this.

i) Faro had the worst return on capital post and EBIT margin for most of the decade.

ii) Faro was generally among the bottom 2 for the free cash flow for most of the review period.

iii) In terms of the quantum of EPS, Faro had the worst performance over the past decade. It also had a reduction in its EPS from 2015 to 2024. While Topcon Corp's EPS also shrank from 2015 to 2024, Faro had a more severe shrinkage. Note that the EPS for the other panel members grew over the past decade.

More importantly, the peer analysis points to the potential return that the company could deliver. Looking at Chart 3, I posit that the best that could be achieved is a consistent 15% return on capital.

Financial position

I consider Faro as moderately sound financially, as its positive points were offset by its negative one. The positive points were:

i) It had $103 million in cash as of the end of March 2025, equivalent to only about 21 % of its total assets.

ii) It has a Debt Equity ratio of 35 % as of March 2025. This was the past decade high.

iii) From 2015 to 2024, it generated positive cash flow from operations for only 8 out of the 10 years. But it had a good cash flow conversion ratio. Over this period, it generated $ 130 million in cash flow from operations compared to its cumulative loss of $ 180 million.

iv) Over the past decade, the average annual Reinvestment was $ 16 million. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

Over the long run, the Reinvestment rate should follow that from the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate.

Based on its March 2025 total invested capital of $250 million and assuming a 15% ROE (to be among the peer best over the past decade), we would have a $37.5 million profit. We would then have a 43% Reinvestment rate. Considering that this included acquisitions, I would consider this acceptable.

Its key negative point was its poor capital allocation plan as per Table 2. The cash flow from operations was not enough to cover its acquisitions and CAPEX.

The company has stated that it would continue to pursue acquisitions beyond 2024. As such, I do not expect a significant change to the historical cash flow picture.

Faro’s financial position is moderately sound, but it does not yet offer the flexibility needed for aggressive self-funded expansion. Its turnaround depends on converting past acquisitions into high-return assets, improving operating leverage, and exercising greater capital allocation discipline. Without these, the firm risks remaining trapped in a cycle of low returns and externally funded growth.

Valuation

My analysis shows that the company is undergoing a turnaround. In determining the potential value of Faro, I assumed that it would be able to grow its revenue, expand its margin, and control its cost to deliver a 15% return on capital in 2025.

The key assumptions of my valuation were thus:

i) A 15 % return on capital in perpetuity at a long-term GDP growth rate of 4%.

ii) A 25% nominal tax rate.

iii) A Reinvestment rate based on the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate

iv) A WACC based on the average WACC of its peers. As the peers operate in different countries, the average WACC should cover the global risks in line with Faro's global operations.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of $ 38 per share. This is below the sale price of $ 44 per share.

Valuation model

I valued Faro based on the single-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) valuation model as shown in Table 3. The WACC was based on the Google Search for the respective peer WACC by GuruFocus, as summarized in Table 4.

Risks and limitations

My valuation assumes that Faro would reach the 15% ROIC immediately. In 2024, the company only delivered a 2.4% ROIC. This 15% ROIC would require a significant revenue growth, some margin expansion, and some lower fixed cost margin. This would be a tall order for Faro.

Despite the optimistic picture, the intrinsic value is less than AMETEK's offer price. The shareholders made the correct decision in selling their shares.

Conclusion

Faro's decade-long transformation was ambitious to position the company in structurally attractive end markets, such as 3D scanning, digital twins, and spatial analytics.

Despite operational improvements and some signs of margin recovery in early 2025, Faro struggled with chronic losses, weak return on capital, and a history of capital allocation that failed to generate sufficient shareholder value. At the same time, it consistently underperformed relative to peers.

To turnaround in a sustainable manner, the company needed to simultaneously grow revenue, expand margins, and contain fixed costs. I think this is an uphill battle that remained largely aspirational.

The $ 44 per share acquisition by AMETEK offered a clean exit at a premium to Faro’s intrinsic value under a reasonable base-case scenario. Rather than waiting for an uncertain and protracted turnaround, shareholders can crystallize more than the intrinsic value through the sale.

Viewed through a fundamental investing lens, Faro is a reminder that strategic pivots, even into promising markets, require not only vision. It also requires disciplined execution and effective capital deployment. Without these, even the most compelling narratives can fail to translate into shareholder returns.

data.title: Talen Energy: The Street Chases AI Chips, I'm Buying The Power Behind Them
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Artificial intelligence is creating a new economy, but few are asking the question "Where will all the energy to power it come from?"

During the gold rush (of the 1800s), the people who really got rich were those who sold picks (or jeans)

In this new gold rush, the companies that supply chips, GPUs, and servers are obviously the ones in the spotlight. But those who provide electricity in a consistent, reliable, immediately scalable way are not considered until later. The storytelling is weaker

Talen Energy (NASDAQ:TLN) is the perfect candidate to fill this void: it is an independent energy company with a dominant presence in the PJM market, well advanced in its transformation from a traditional utility to an integrated platform to serve the AI economy. The market still assesses it as a “nuclear utility.” In reality, it is a strategic provider for AI-hungry data centers, with a replicable business model and accelerating cash flow generation.

My thesis is simple: TLN is one of the best risk/reward opportunities in the AI infrastructure sector today. The acquisition of two new gas-combined power plants and the expansion of the Amazon deal are concrete evidence that the story has just begun.

Company Overview

Talen Energy is a leading independent power producer in the United States, headquartered in Houston. It owns and operates about 10.7 GW of installed capacity, including 2.2 GW nuclear, with operational focus in the Mid-Atlantic (PJM) and Montana.

The operational core is the Susquehanna power plant in Pennsylvania, which provides long-term carbon-free power to Amazon Web Services (AWS) under a contract extended through 2042 for up to 1,920 MW.

Over the past 12 months, Talen has completely restructured its business model: rationalizing legacy assets, reducing costs, and reinvesting in highly efficient baseload infrastructure.

Recent Developments: The Deal That Changes Everything

On July 17, 2025, Talen announced the $3.5 billion (net enterprise value, already including $300M tax benefit) purchase of two state-of-the-art CCGT gas-fired power plants:

  • Moxie Freedom (1.045 MW, Pennsylvania, near Susquehanna)
  • Guernsey (1,836 MW, Ohio, rapidly expanding data center area)

According to information posted on the company's July 17 website, the transaction:

  • Adds around 3 GW of capacity with an average heat rate of 6,550 Btu/kWh (among the lowest in PJM)
  • implies an EV/EBITDA 2026 multiple of 6.7x (including tax benefits)
  • Will be accretive by more than 40% on FCF per share as early as 2026

In parallel, the contract extension with Amazon a few weeks ago marks another turning point. AWS will receive up to 1,920 MW of carbon-free power from the Susquehanna nuclear power plant, with the possibility of SMRs (small modular reactors) in the long term. This reduces revenue volatility and makes Talen a partner of choice for big tech.

Earnings & Guidance

In Q1 2025, Talen Energy reported Adjusted EBITDA of $200 million and Adjusted Free Cash Flow of $87 million, beating estimates. Despite a negative GAAP result (-135 million dollar), operating generation remains fully positive and consistent with a capital-intensive business that has just begun to scale toward the most powerful growth cycle in its history.

The GAAP figure in my view is in fact misleading because it includes nonrecurring accounting impacts such as unrealized losses on derivatives and the non-recurrence of the $324 million gain recorded in Q1 2024 for the sale of the AWS data campus. But no serious investor evaluates an IPP like Talen on the basis of GAAP net income; rather, they look at cash generation, and that remains solid.

The company confirmed and narrowed 2025 guidance:

  • Adjusted EBITDA expected between $975 and $1,125 million
  • Free Cash Flow estimated between $450 and $540 million

Important numbers, but again conservative because they do not include the accretionary effect of the newly acquired power plants (Guernsey and Moxie Freedom), whose contribution will be seen from 2026. So we have potential that has not yet been expressed. Let's keep that in mind.

On the operational side, total generation also grew to 9.7 TWh, an improvement from 8.1 TWh in Q1 2024. Forty-six percent was carbon-free, coming from the Susquehanna nuclear power plant, which remains the beating heart of the 20-year agreement with Amazon

I personally really appreciate this mix (stable nuclear base + highly profitable gas expansion) because it becomes a kind of “natural hedge” against AI demand volatility.

Interesting note to put on the table is the fact that the company announced that since early 2024 it has repurchased about 23% of its outstanding shares, spending $2 billion. In Q1 alone, it repurchased 452,130 shares for $83 million. This is an important signal because it means Talen is not only generating cash, but aggressively returning it to shareholders.

Share Repurchases Since the start of 2024, we have repurchased approximately 14 million shares, or 23% of our outstanding shares, for a total of approximately $2 billion, with $995 million remaining under our share repurchase program through year-end 2026. During the first quarter 2025, we repurchased 452,130 shares of stock for a total of $83 million. All share repurchase amounts exclude transaction costs.

These numbers demonstrate that Talen has completed its financial turnaround, and has entered full expansion mode, with cash flow, leverage under control (net leverage expected at 2.6x in 2025), and an energy growth pipeline among the most attractive in the industry.

Valuation Supporting the Rating

Those looking at Talen Energy's current multiples might turn their noses up at it. I would too, it is clear and obvious.

  • EV/EBITDA trailing above 14x.
  • P/E 30 or so

But this static picture is not only dated but misleading.

Talen's valuation should be read in projection, not on an accounting basis. The numbers for 2024 and the first half of 2025 tell the end of a cycle: the post-emergency and post-restructuring cycle. What we are entering now is a new operating cycle, one in which the company is turning on strategic, ultra-high-margin assets just as AI energy demand is soaring.

According to Bloomberg's most up-to-date estimates, in 2026:

  • EBITDA $1.539 billion
  • Free Cash Flow $855.9 million
  • Net Debt $2.924 billion
  • EV/EBITDA fwd 2026 at 10.83x
  • P/FCF fwd 2026 13.2x
  • Estimated FCF Yield 7.5%

Now let's do the math. If we assume a target multiple of 9x EV/EBITDA, entirely reasonable for an operator with AI-linked contracts, zero carbon energy, and growing operating margin:

  • EV = 9 × 1.539B = 13.851B
  • Equity Value = 13.851B − 2.924B = 10.927B
  • Fair Value = 10.927B​ / 32M = $341 per share

So, Talen today trades at a 10-15% discount to a base-case fair value, which it does not incorporate:

  • No upside from small modular reactors (SMR)
  • No FCF acceleration from further buyback
  • No green-premium revaluation from U.S. energy policies

The narrative has fallen behind. Estimates need to be updated:

But no one has fully priced in the structural asymmetry yet.

From my perspective, the market still does not price Talen correctly: it is now a cash-rich, AI-linked energy infrastructure with favorable geographic and regulatory positioning.

In a market that pays 50-70x the profits of chipmakers and AI software, here we have a critical data center power provider, at 11.5x P/FCF. With visibility, pricing power, and structural buyback potential. This is the kind of asymmetry I want to have in my portfolio.

Personally, I don't see many companies in the market that can offer:

  • Real growth +80% EBITDA in two years
  • FCF yield >7%
  • Key positioning in the AI-infrastructure transition
  • Regulatory protection on the nuclear side
  • Pricing power from capacity shortages on the PJM
  • All, at 13x FCF forward

This is the very definition of market inefficiency. And until the rerating is completed, this inefficiency is our profit margin.

Importantly, Bloomberg's current estimates do not yet fully incorporate recent developments, including the CCGT maxi-acquisition, the expanded PPA with Amazon, and the SMR pipeline. This means that our current fair value of $341 should be considered conservative, with real room for rerating to $375-$400 as the consensus is updated post-Q2.

Risks and Opportunities

Let's start with the risks, because ignoring them would be naive.

  1. Talen has a capital structure that, while improving, still remains to be watched: Net Debt/EBITDA 2026 will be around 1.9x, but only after integration of the new plants. If M&A slips or 2026 EBITDA disappoints, the credit profile could come under pressure again. In addition, there is the issue of maturing debt: about $1.5 billion needs to be refinanced in the next 12 months, according to Bloomberg. With high rates, the cost of capital is a real risk.
  2. Nuclear-related regulatory and political risk is not to be underestimated. Should public opinion or congressional direction change after 2026, Talen, which has focused heavily on zero-emission energy, could face repercussions in terms of permitting, incentives, or regulatory visibility. To be watched, but today the scenario is favorable (and bipartisan).
  3. Finally, dependence on the PJM market-a concentration that makes Talen a geographically focused asset. This can be a competitive advantage (where there is a shortage), but also a vulnerability in case of extraordinary events, restrictive policies, or changes in local demand.

On the other side of the coin, opportunity.

The opportunity is colossal. The AI power gap is real and structural, and Talen is among the very few U.S. operators with:

  • available capacity,
  • carbon-free power already in place,
  • 20 year contracts with big tech.

The PPA with Amazon is just the first example: other companies (Meta, Microsoft, Oracle) will seek the same kind of solution. And those who have available and flexible energy at the right nodes can write the prices. Talen is in this position.

In addition, the potential for SMR (modular reactor) development and power upgrades at Susquehanna is yet to be exploited. No analysts are yet modeling it in flows, but it could represent the “surprise” upside of 2026-2027.

So it is true, Talen has risks to monitor (leverage, execution, PJM exposure), but the opportunities (especially for an investor looking at 12-36 months) are more concrete and less discounted in price. And that is precisely why the stock today, for me, deserves a Strong Buy.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Talen Energy is not just any utility. Today, utility is becoming a critical infrastructure asset in a world that is reshaping the very concept of energy. Big tech needs chips. Chips need power. And power, real power comes from operators like Talen.

The market is still treating it as a leveraged cyclical. But under the surface there is a new model, which is 20-year contracts, physical capacity in the right nodes, expanding margins, and cash coming in already. With Amazon, it is just the beginning. Gas-fired power plants are the bridge. The SMR is the free option.

With an FCF yield near 9%, a valuation still compressed, and one of the best risk/return profiles in the infrastructure sector, Talen is a rational bet in a world that looks only at chips and tech.

Strong Buy.

data.title: The Fed Should Pause Again
data.description:
The Federal Open Market Committee meets again on July 29-30, and the consensus expectation is that the Committee will once again hold the target Fed funds rate at the current range of 4.0 – 4.25 percent. Unlike recent decisions, though, this one may not be unanimous. A small subset of the FOMC’s twelve voting members has been quite vocal in recent weeks about the desirability of a July rate cut. Perhaps unsurprisingly, this strain of Fedspeak has played out in the context of the increasingly strident rhetoric from the White House urging (inadvisably and inappropriately) for interest rates to be slashed. To appreciate why a pause is the right move for the moment, let’s consider a forty-year history of FOMC interest rate decisions and the economic conditions in which those decisions were made.

Jobs and Prices

The Fed has two jobs, made explicit in its dual mandate: stable prices and full employment. When a hot economy threatens to unleash higher inflation, it’s time to raise interest rates and cool things off. Conversely, when the unemployment rate starts to trend sharply upwards, then a policy of rate cuts is appropriate as a way to stimulate the economy and generate job growth. Simple, right? Not really. The Fed is not in possession of a crystal ball any more than you or we are. The FOMC has to work with whatever data it has to figure out how dire the situation is and when the right time is to execute the decision. As the chart below shows, however, the Committee has done a pretty good job of this over the past forty years, with each of the four major rate cut cycles coinciding with an economic recession.

Those rate-cut decisions in 1990, 2000, 2007 and 2019, respectively, look even more prescient when considered in conjunction with the employment circumstances of the time. The unemployment rate rose to a maximum level of 7.7 percent, 6.1 percent, 9.9 percent and 14.8 percent over the course of those recessions. The Fed fulfilled its mandate by following policies aimed at bringing unemployment back down (which, in each case, it did).

When the Fed Stayed Put

And then there was the case when the Fed paused. This followed the rate hikes of 1994 (which caught financial markets by surprise), when the Fed took preemptive measures to head off what it feared could be a return of higher inflation. By April of 1995, the Fed funds rate was at 6.0 percent. There was a slight uptick in unemployment a couple months later, and the Fed brought rates slightly lower, similar to what the Committee did last September in easing back from the maximum range this cycle of 5.25 – 5.5 percent.

And then – nothing, for two years. In the mid-1990s, unemployment continued falling, and growth perked up without an undue amount of attendant inflation. Given the data at hand, the Fed didn’t need to act again until September 1998, when the Russian debt crisis and the ensuing collapse of the Long-Term Credit Management hedge fund brought fears of a systemic financial market crash (those fears proved short-lived, and the Fed resumed raising rates in 1999).

And now back to the upcoming July 30 FOMC decision. The unemployment rate is currently 4.1 percent, and it has been close to this level for several months, along with a fairly stable level of gains in nonfarm payrolls. Meanwhile, the core Consumer Price Index rate is 2.9 percent, still meaningfully above the 2.0 percent target rate. In the CPI report that came out this past Tuesday, it was possible to see incipient indications of the tariff effect on certain categories of goods such as apparel and home furnishings. The full inflationary effect of tariffs remains an unknown variable.

In terms of that dual mandate of prices and jobs, then, the data available today continue to support a strategy of “better safe than sorry.” Hold for now and see what the price and jobs data say as the calendar heads into the next FOMC meeting in September.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How Hims & Hers Health Is Quietly Changing Healthcare
data.description:
Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) is evolving from a niche direct-to-consumer (DTC) health brand into a full-stack digital health platform. With 95% recurring revenue, breakeven profits, and personalized care at scale, it presents a rare combination of durability, upside optionality, and execution visibility.

Hims will likely be described merely as a high-growth “GLP-1” darling of the telehealth era, yet its leadership argues that its own story is one of building a consumer health platform. As a 2.4 million-subscriber powerhouse, the company likens itself to a “Netflix” of personalized care, using each subscriber's data to refine treatments and to accelerate cross-specialty sales. Such platform-minded thinking directly counterposes the market's obsession with headline growth alone.

Hims & Hers inhabits the telehealth and digital health ecosystems, which are large multi-hundred-billion-dollar opportunity spaces. The global telehealth market had attained approximately $123 billion in the year 2024 and was forecast to reach up to $455 billion in the year 2030, suggesting a 24.7% CAGR driven by penetrated smartphones, broadband growth, as well as increased consumer adoption. North America dominates with close to half the market, aligning directly with Hims’ U.S.-focused revenue base.

The broader digital health landscape, which includes wearables, remote diagnostics, and AI-powered tools, is even larger, valued at about $289 billion in 2024 and expected to swell to $946 billion by 2030, growing at a 22% CAGR. Telehealth continues as the prime segment, yet surrounding therapy and diagnostic segments offer more avenues for growth.

With 2.4 million subscribers in focused verticals including dermatology, mental health, sexual health, and weight management, Hims owns an estimated 50% share of primary-market digital care providers across these niches. These verticals, however, represent only a sliver of the telehealth and digital health opportunities. As Hims introduces chronic-care services, such as hormone therapy, peptide-based longevity therapies, and diagnostic lab work, the company’s serviceable addressable market could meaningfully expand by 2030. Through planned global rollout, e.g., through its planned acquisition of ZAVA, Hims will be poised to capture scalable growth in multiple high-potential verticals.

From ED to Endocrinology: How Hims Is Rebuilding Primary Care, One Subscription at a Time

Hims began life as a direct-to-consumer platform focusing on men's sexual health and hair loss, then diversified into women's health. The core business model throughout has been recurring month-end shipment charging by subscription to treat on. The company today serves five core specialties, men's and women's dermatology, sexual health, weight loss, and mental health, in a centralized digital platform. Virtually all its sales are on subscription, although one-time wholesale shipment sales on a de minimis basis exist. Subscriptions have grown by more than one third year to year, spurred by expansion into new therapy lines like GLP-1 weight loss therapies, generic liraglutide, and branded formulations like Wegovy alongside coaching and nutrition counseling.

This expansion declares an intentional expansion from lower-acuity segments to higher, chronic-care segments, extending its shelves on its virtual store. Additionally, the firm has most recently finalized buying acquisitions in at-home lab testing and peptide drug manufacturing. These assets prime Hims & Hers to offer their own diagnostics and therapies in house as opposed to depending on outside partners for these services. The end goal is a one-stop digital mall of healthcare: consumers order a lab test, go to a doc via the app, and receive customized medications filled from an internal pharmacy, all on one streamlined patient journey.

Even more crucial, this change in structures facilitates wider offerings that mesh testing and treatments for hormone-based conditions like low testosterone and menopause, segments to come by year's end. Along the journey, the company remained strict to its subscription DNA. Customers choose a cadence on repeat delivery, and customization becomes more baked-in; 60% choose customized approaches, many bundling treatments by multiple conditions. The more breadth to offerings, the more the company aims to cross-sell, leveraging each customer's data to further customize care. The plan's not merely upselling; it's to build a stickiest relationship that widens the moat by one more specialty at a time.

The Subscription Compounder: How Hims Is Turning Retention into Recurring Gold

The Hims & Hers revenue mix is very recurring, offering predictability and an obvious long-term compounding avenue to value. Online subscriber average monthly revenue rose by more than 50% year over year during the most recent quarter, a testimonial to upselling and individualized treatment programs.

As revenues surged 111% during the latest quarter, however, the leadership looks instead to retention rates that hover near 85% and to building multi-specialty ecosystem-forming. That, namely, is how Hims & Hers positions itself not merely as a one-hit high growth stock but an ongoing multi-specialty subscriber engine, a contrarian description that places its focus on monetization quality and on network effects rather than pure top-line momentum.

Similarly, profitability lines are healthy. Net income margins have swung to positive ground, and adjusted EBITDA margins improve as the subscriber base expands. Q1 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $25.6 million, a 255% YoY increase, with net income at $11.6 million versus a $10.1 million loss last year. Healthy operating cash flow validates leverage on this growth to tangible return on a sustained basis.

Marketing cost, which once took a gigantic percentage of revenue, shows meaningful leverage while organic acquisition continues to enhance and retention solidifies. Customer payback that still remains below one year provides prudent deployment of capital, while operating and support costs continue to decline as a percentage of revenue. Q1 CAC remained ~$93, while ARPU exceeded $70, implying a payback period under 5 months.

Most importantly, this trajectory insinuates that the firm is cultivating a virtuous cycle rather than concealing weakness behind spend that's pushy and hard to sustain. Each new specialty and personalized service should enhance retention and boost lifetime value, funding the next marketing spend, and so on. It's an intelligent hedge: though weight-reduction injectables come at an initially higher cost and thinner gross margins, supporting them in parallel to bundled coaching and follow-through care helps to sustain drop-off rates below industry-standard churn.

Also, preliminary evidence of scale efficiency comes from increased free cash flow and steady margin expansion. Free cash flow was $21.6 million in Q1 2025, up from $3.2 million YoY. The cash conversion is good, and the capacity to invest this back into automating and satisfying capacity by the management should sustain operating leverage while volumes build up. Though most digital players in healthcare are still yet to cross the break-even threshold level of EBITDA, Hims & Hers is on a path to sustained profitability. Cumulative net cash now exceeds $200 million, with no debt obligations.

Beyond the Clinic: How Hims Is Outbuilding Teladoc with a Full-Stack Consumer Health Engine

Competing against established telehealth players and digital-first incumbents, Hims & Hers is building a differentiated product. Telehealth giants like Teladoc have traditionally focused on enterprise plans and insurer networks. However, according to Teladoc’s 2024 10-K, their DTC expansion has lagged, reflecting challenges in monetizing self-pay users. Whereas insurance-based models failed to translate insurance-derived attributes like scale and cost savings to pay-out-of-pocket, convenience, and discretion, those are traits that self-payment consumers value its platform and are difficult to replicate.

What sets the company apart is its technical and operating depth. Whereas other direct-to-consumer startups are relying on outsourced fulfillment and blanket vendor relationships, Hims & Hers is building a proprietary stack: licensed pharmacy, an in-house lab diagnostic capability, and then peptide synthesis. The vertical integration not only enables quality control, however, but speeds up speed to market on new therapies, something its competitors won’t easily match without commensurate capital expenditures.

Then there's the company's bet on personalization as a moat. AI-enabled products like its MedMatch platform try to tighten care recommendations incrementally with each new data point. That translates to each interaction, a workup on the lab, a change on dosage, or an outcome on therapy, all cycle back to making the overall ecosystem stronger, boosting the stickiness on the subscription offering. Its digital competitors like GoodRx or Ro offer one-point products without the same network effects or service richness.

Novo Breakup: A Strategic Blow, Not a Knockout

Hims & Hers’ abrupt June 2025 breakup with Novo Nordisk was a sudden reversal of the previously hailed importance of the deal as a significant cred-building move to the company’s entry into chronic weight care. Just months after celebrating the partnership to offer branded GLP-1 therapies like Wegovy through the platform, Novo abruptly terminated the deal, claiming Hims continued selling of compounded semaglutide outside FDA windows of shortages.

The pharma giant accused Hims of patient safety betrayal and misrepresentation of the character of the compounded products it sold, while Hims maintained it remained within FDA exemptions and attempted to offer affordable, customized alternatives. The split caused a 35% decline in the stock price and eliminated more than $5 billion of market capitalization, an acute symptom of the reputational risk at the core of striking the balance of regulatory compliance and commercial innovation in direct-to-patient healthcare.

Despite the high-profile split and related legal scrutiny, including shareholder lawsuits alleging a failure to disclose, Hims seeks to turn quickly. It has re-committed to its position regarding disclosure of compound sourcing, accelerated development of the proprietary injectable formulation of semaglutide, and diversified its self-managed pipeline of supplies, including moves into overseas territories like Canada.

While the Novo partnership had the benefit of branding firepower as well as supply credibility, the breakup of the same removes a dependency and re-centers Hims’ model back around full-stack autonomy. The incident, however, puts a spotlight on the tightrope Hims walks, appealing to the convenience and cost-consciousness of the end-consumer without stoking conflict across legacy paradigms of pharma. Whether it accelerates Hims’ chronic care hopes or not will be dependent upon how successfully it can re-establish trust, not just among regulators, but among the market.

Risk/Reward Analysis: Is HIMS Overpriced or Under-appreciated?

HIMS trades at a high forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 34.6x and EV/Sales of 4.7x at $51.85, multiples that screen as expensive compared to traditional healthcare peers. But those multiples neglect the core of the HIMS story as an extremely lean, software-like growth engine in health for the consumer. FY2025 revenue should increase 56–63% to $2.3–$2.4 billion, as the blended EBITDA margins increase to 13–14%, generating ~$315 million of future EBITDA. On that foundation, the company valuation puts a high value on durability as opposed to growth.

What accounts for the premium? First, subscription revenue comprises ~95% of the mix, monthly average revenue per subscriber up more than 50% year-on-year, and retention greater than 85%, all strong signals of growing lifetime value. Second, very few pure-play digital health platforms have reached break-even EBITDA, let alone 14% margins at this scale.

Hims is showing growth and profitability while broadening the serviceable addressable market through different verticals like weight loss, hormone therapy, and mental health. If the company achieves revenue growth of a modest 22% CAGR, it would go up from the FY2025 guided midpoint of $2.35 billion to over $4.27 billion by FY2028. If it achieves scaling operating margins and reaches $600 million EBITDA, applying a 35x EV/EBITDA multiple would result in a $21 billion enterprise value by FY2028.

At the current enterprise value of $11.35 billion, that scenario would come out to a ballpark double three-year upside. And that is with base-case growth and profitability assumed for the most part. In short, the case for Hims' fundamentals is strong, and the stock is a good risk/reward at these levels.

Hims is quietly constructing the first vertically integrated, consumer-centric healthcare platform with software-like economics, integrating 95% recurring revenue, increasing ARPS, and more than 85% retention into a compounding machine. The majority of digital health peers remain unprofitable or dependent upon enterprise payors, while Hims is already breakeven with 14% EBITDA margins and self-funded growth. This builds in a safety net: the losses are relatively contained because the business is not burning capital, there is no debt risk, and there is operating leverage.

On the other hand, the upside is great. If Hims surpasses the base case, perhaps by entering new verticals such as hormones and peptides, globalizing through ZAVA, or re-rating through greater margin or platform premium, it’s easy for it to go beyond the implied $12 billion EV. That’s the kind of profile for a high-upside, well-backed platform investment with limited downside, a floor underpinned by strong fundamentals, and a ceiling that increases with execution. One is buying not only today’s momentum, but the optionality of a scalable digital infrastructure for personalized care across chronic, behavioral, and wellness areas.

Regulatory Whiplash Could Threaten Hims’ Growth Engine

Regulatory attention is the most significant threat facing Hims & Hers since the breakup with Novo Nordisk in June 2025. The conflict was related to the continuing sale by Hims compounded semaglutide in non-shortage periods as delineated by the FDA, which placed the company under the spotlight for patient safety and sent the stock plummeting by 35%.

This event further heightens how much Hims’ expansion is contingent upon addressing shifting FDA guidance for compounded drugs. If the regulators increase restrictions for GLP-1 compounding or expand the scope of inquiry, this could materially affect Hims’ fastest-growing revenue stream and erode confidence with customers and partners alike.

As a DTC pharma, Hims accepts all regulatory and legal risk with no recourse granted by insurers or PBMs. Transparency and proactive compliance are the new game plan for creating and maintaining momentum and sidestepping coming jolts.

Conclusion

Platform scale, recurring revenue, and breakeven growth are combined by Hims & Hers and offer investors a unique opportunity within digital health with defensible fundamentals, expanding TAM, and upside through execution-driven re-rating.

data.title: Northern Dynasty Minerals Seeks A Summary Judgment After EPA Settlement Talks Stall
data.description:
Northern Dynasty Minerals (NYSE:NAK) was dealt a blow with the inability to reach a settlement with the EPA around withdrawing its veto. Northern Dynasty has now filed a motion in the Federal District Court in Alaska seeking a summary judgment in its litigation over the veto. While there is still a chance that the veto will be overturned in the courts, it would have been significantly better for Northern Dynasty if it was able to reach a settlement.

That being said, I have moved to a near-term neutral outlook on the stock based on trading value. It is currently pricing in a roughly 20% to 25% chance of the veto being removed through court actions, which seems generally reasonable to me.

I am still more skeptical about Pebble in the longer-term since there are still financing issues and continued legal battles even if Northern Dynasty can achieve a near-term court victory.

This report uses US dollars as the base currency, along with an exchange rate of CAD$1.37 to US$1.00.

EPA's Stance

Northern Dynasty's July 4 announcement that it was negotiating with the EPA to explore a potential settlement helped push its stock up by over 60% by July 16.

I viewed this announcement as being a modest positive, but based on the wording, didn't consider it to be a done deal. Words such as "negotiating to explore a potential settlement" left a reasonable amount of doubt that the veto would be voluntarily withdrawn.

In the end, after months of abeyances, Northern Dynasty and the EPA were unable to reach a settlement and it appears that the EPA will attempt to defend the veto in the courts.

Court Case

Northern Dynasty is now seeking a summary judgment in its litigation over the veto, attempting to move matters ahead more quickly.

The court case is being overseen by Judge Sharon Gleason. Judge Gleason was appointed by President Obama and has ruled both for and against environmental groups before.

With the EPA apparently defending the veto, I am assuming 20% odds of success for Northern Dynasty. Judge Gleason is seen as fair, but she seems to be politically at least somewhat to the left of President Trump's EPA.

The case can be appealed to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. Historically, 81% of Judge Gleason's decisions are upheld on appeal.

Insider Transactions

Many Northern Dynasty insiders reported selling shares during this last week, with so far over 3 million shares reported sold at roughly around the mid-$2 to $3 range (in CAD).

Some of the sales were connected to recently exercised options, as Northern Dynasty had approximately 6.7 million options (with a CAD$2.01 exercise price) expiring on July 17, 2025. The 6.7 million options include those owned by non-insiders who would not need to report their transactions.

This insider selling was not that surprising given that I believed Northern Dynasty's near-term upside was a bit over US$2 per share if there was a veto removal. Northern Dynasty's share price closed at US$2.30 on July 16, which basically fully priced in a veto removal despite there still being uncertainty about what would happen.

Notes On Valuation

In terms of near-term prices, I continue to believe that a veto removal would result in a US$2+ share price for Northern Dynasty, while a full court loss could drop its share to around US$0.60 per share.

A 20% chance of success in the courts would thus result in a expected value in the US$0.90s per share.

Northern Dynasty should have enough cash to last into 2027 currently, after the receipt of the third US$12 million tranche payment earlier this year, and the proceeds from exercised options.

Thus I am moving to a near-term neutral outlook on Northern Dynasty as a trading stock, although I am still more skeptical about its longer-term value.

This is a very lengthy process, as I had noted back in 2020 when I gave Pebble a 20% chance of getting into production by the end of 2029. Even with a near-term court victory, Pebble won't be able get into production during this decade now.

Conclusion

Northern Dynasty's share price took a big hit after it was unable to come to a settlement agreement with the EPA. Its share price was essentially pricing in a 100% chance of success, but now I would estimate its odds at around 20% around succeeding through near-term court actions.

I am now neutral on Northern Dynasty at around US$1 per share based on my estimated odds of court success.

Longer-term I am more skeptical of Northern Dynasty's value since it still faces other hurdles in making Pebble into a producing mine. I think there is a significant chance that the project is in a similar spot several years from now.

DIVZ strategy

It has a portfolio of 30 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.19%, and an expense ratio of 0.65%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described in the prospectus by TrueShares, the fund

…seeks to achieve its investment objective by purchasing 25-35 stocks of companies that pay dividends and expect to grow the dividends over time and are trading at attractive valuations.

Selection criteria include: above-average dividend yields, ability and commitment to sustain and grow dividends; management team quality, compelling free cash flow growth and valuation ratios.

data.title: Flipping Burgers - McDonald's And Restaurant Brands International Revisited
data.description:
McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) is a company that needs no introduction due to a brand that is loud and proudly represented worldwide. Restaurant Brands International (NYSE:QSR) is less well known as a stock, but flagship brands like Burger King and Tim Hortons are easily recognisable.

While each company has its nuances, and pro’s and con’s, I see them as highly comparable competitors that can be traded as a pair. I covered this thesis in an article in April this year in which I compared the two firms in terms of fundamentals, but more importantly valuation.

The summary was that both are high-quality companies, which can fit well in a long term diversified portfolio as ‘buy and hold’ stocks. However, as the valuations between the two diverge, my preferred strategy is to switch positions when the valuations dictate. MCD is a great long term value creator, and has rewarded shareholders well over the long term. QSR has been much more volatile, has higher leverage, but offers faster growth. QSR share price fluctuations over time have created regular buying opportunities.

I outlined a simple “Flipping Burger” strategy, to capitalise on the valuation deviation that I have observed. Check out the article in the link above for the full story.

A quarter on, I am checking in on the pair.

Price Performance

So far the thesis on valuation reversion has played out. QSR has appreciated by 9.4%, while MCD has declined by 5.2%, nearly a 15% outperformance by QSR.

Valuation

Price to earnings TTM shows the picture well. With the high debt load, broader product base, and lower global brand recognition QSR should trade at a discount to MCD. A 10% P/E multiple discount seems like an appropriate gap. On this metric, the pair seems to be fairly valued on a comparative basis.

However on a forward P/E basis, QSR still appears to offer better value, due to the more positive earnings outlook than MCD.

What’s behind this is the analyst forward earnings estimates.

For QSR, the year on year earnings growth for the upcoming quarter is over 12%. 2 year forward earnings estimates for 2025 and 2026 are for 10.14% growth, compared to a more modest 6.4% earnings growth estimated for MCD. The table below is from Seeking Alpha data.

The main drivers for this at QSR are the progress in the turnaround of the Burger King brand under the 'Reclaim the Fire' initiative, continued growth of the international segment, and a continued tailwind at home in Canada with the 'Canada First' shift in consumer preferences to support local brands in the light of the ongoing U.S. trade tensions.

Turning to price to cash flow, the valuation range is tighter, with MCD coming out ahead by a margin of around 5%. This reflects a stronger cash flow yield being generated by MCD.

QSR FCF has fluctuated with the more volatile earnings over the years. While we don't have forward FCF guidance, the improving overall sales and margins should also reflect in a continuation of the FCF improvement that can be seen over the last reporting year.

Conversely, MCD saw a slight deterioration in FCF over the 2023/2024 period. This is likely the result of higher capex invested via the 'Accelerate The Arches' initiative. Higher capex can be expected through 2025 due to the continued investment, resulting in worsening FCF, with management banking on sales growth the follow on from the capex investment in 2026, and improve FCF.

In a nutshell I'd expect to see QSR FCF improve on a forward basis, and MCD FCF decline, resulting in a wider discount in QSR valuation in terms of price to forward FCF than is shown above.

QSR has a stronger franchise vs owned store mic than MCD, which enables more of the investment costs in funding sales and store improvement to be passed through to franchisees. For MCD, sales growth conversion to FCF is likely to be less efficient than QSR.

The Analyst View

Overall, both SA Analysts and SA Quant rate MCD as a hold, with poor scores for valuation and growth, offset by profitability and positive earnings revisions.

Wall St analysts have MCD as a Buy.

Wall St average price target is for a 10.7% upside. Note the large deviation between the high and the low price target.

QSR enjoys a much more positive analyst sentiment, with Buy ratings from all three sources.

SA quant scores QSR higher across most categories. Valuation is only rated a C-, which is one to watch.

Wall St has only a slightly higher price target for QSR than for MCD, with an 11.2% average price target. The high vs low deviation is also pronounced.

Conclusion

So far the strategy to flip from MCD to QSR has worked out well, generating 15% alpha over the last quarter. As one has drifted up, and the other drifted down, the valuation gap has narrowed, but is still pronounced on both TTM and forward earnings.

While FCF the valuation for MCD is now below that of QSR on a TTM basis, I expect to see the FCF for MCD to reduce, and for QSR to accelerate over the remainder of 2025.

As such, I don't think that it is time to flip the pair just yet. I will be watching the Q2 earnings of each for updates on the valuation metrics and outlook. If I see the MCD premium erode to just 10% across the metric, this will be a trigger to flip from QSR to MCD.

data.title: Leonardo DRS: Impressive Company That's Highly Valued
data.description:
Introduction

Leonardo DRS, Inc. (NASDAQ:DRS) is a defense technology company based in Arlington, Virginia. The company operates in Advanced Sensing and Computing and Integrated Mission Systems segments. The first segment focuses on building a suite of products that can be called "real time situational awareness" solutions which allow the military to collect data from many different platforms and sources and make effective decisions quickly. The second segment actually utilizes a lot of technology developed by the first segment in order to offer a integrated package of defense solutions that utilize both land and naval resources

Business Overview

The company designs, develops, and manufactures integrated solutions for the military, intelligence services, and other military contractors. The company was originally founded in the 1960s during the Cold War, and its main products mainly focused on detection and disruption of enemy submarines which was a vital task at the time. Now the company is using its many years of experience in defensive technology development to create more modern and integrative solutions.

The company uses a "platform-agnostic" approach to its product development which means that its products will work with any platform. The military works with many different contractors which creates a large number of platforms to deal with. Often times contractors' solutions will only work on their own platforms or a limited number of platforms. Leonardo focuses on a more flexible approach where products are designed in a way that allows them to work on multiple different platforms and integrate with other solutions. This is achieved both on the software and hardware side, giving the company a nice moat. Since the company doesn't build a platform of its own, it can focus its efforts on the entire defense ecosystem. This also makes it difficult to replace this company's products, giving it stickiness.

Strategic Focus

The company's strategic focus is wide, flexible, and adaptable but at the same time targeted and focused. How can these two things be true at the same time? Let me explain. The company's strategic focus shifts along with the military's own prioritization. The company periodically looks at what product types are in high demand in a given environment and where most growth opportunities can come from. When picking its strategic target, the company is very flexible and open to going in any direction, but once the target is picked and narrowed, the company shifts its focus entirely there. The company is not shy about canceling products that aren't delivering value in order to focus more on projects that create more value, but it is also open to shifting its priorities if the military's own priorities change. After all, the military is the biggest customer, and that's where the money comes from. If you insist on building products that your customer doesn't want, you will end up having no revenues no matter how great your products might be.

One example of this strategic shift is what the company has been doing for the last few years. It shifted its priorities into four areas: 1) autonomous, 2) dynamic, 3) interconnected, and 4) multi-domain capabilities. These also coincide with the military's own modernization priorities (for example see this document from Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2)), and even though I used the word "coincide" this is no coincidence. The company's leadership actively tries to get feedback from the military through its well-established relationships to inform its strategic decisions. Besides, it's not like the military's new strategic shift towards more interconnectedness and more automation is a secret either.

Ownership Structure

As if this wasn't complicated enough, the company also has a unique ownership structure. At the same time it is both a subsidiary and a separate company. About 80% of the company's shares are owned by an Italian defense company named Leonardo S.p.a. (OTCPK:FINMF). The remaining 20% of the company is publicly traded, thus the ticker of DRS. This means the total number of shares floating in the market is actually only 1/5th of the company's total shares outstanding since Leonardo S.p.A. has no intention of selling any shares for the foreseeable future. This creates a scarcity of shares in the market considering there are very few shares available to buy. This probably helps the company's shares to carry a premium, but I wouldn't say the premium is as big as 1 to 5. This also doesn't mean that the Italian parent company gets to keep 80% of this company's profits. This means it gets to keep 80% of the dividends though since they own 80% of the shares and each share receives a dividend when the company chooses to pay it.

Product Pipeline

The company's pipeline includes many products with fancy futuristic names such as The Battlefield AI Processor (the AIP). The Battlefield AI Processor is similar to the self-driving feature we see in Tesla (TSLA) cars today, but in addition to driving it also includes battle management. It integrates with military vehicles like tanks or drones and collects and processes massive amounts of data that it collects from different tools such as cameras, thermal sensors, microphones, and radars at such a speed much faster than a human possibly could. Then it plans a course of action very quickly to determine what should be done next in line with the new data. Mind you, the data is not just collected and analyzed at one specific time. The data actually constantly flows in a stream, and the tool is constantly analyzing new data as it flows in, just the same way a self-driving vehicle would. It can also identify friendly forces, civilians, and enemies so that accidental casualties can be avoided.

There are also the new CMOSS/SOSA standards and regulations which dictate that electronic tools and components must now share the same connectors, the same power standards, and common software protocols. For example, all radios and computers should have interchangeable charging adapters as much as possible. These new regulations may benefit companies like Leonardo that utilize a platform-agnostic approach as I described above.

Financials

Now let us look at some of the company's metrics. In the last couple years the company's revenues exploded from about $2.5 billion to $3.5 billion. Similarly, the company's margin categories showed significant gains as its gross margin rose from 17% to 23%, operating margin rose from 7.5% to 9.2%, and EBIT margin rose from 8% to 9%. As a result of these, the company's net income rose at a rate higher than 150% since 2022.

The company's growth rate is significantly higher than its peers. The company's revenue growth is more than triple the growth rate of its sector, while its EBITDA growth rate is 200% higher than the sector average, and its EPS growth rate of 25% represents a 400% premium over its peers. Although the company's revenue growth rate slowed down from its 5-year average of 31%, it's still pretty significant at the current rate.

Valuation

However, much of this growth seems to be reflected in the company's valuation. The company trades at a significant premium against its peers as well as its own 5-year average with a current trailing P/E of 48 on a non-GAAP basis and 55 on a GAAP basis, significantly above the sector median of 20 to 24 and the company's own 5-year average of 33. Even if you focus entirely on the PEG metric which accounts for a company's growth rate, we are still getting a 68% premium against peers as the company's current PEG of 2.11 is compared to the sector median of 1.25. A fair value for this company would be a PEG of ~1.5 which would create a balance between its growth and value.

Conclusion

On one hand the company has a bright future with a successful suite of products, strong growth, and a significant technological moat but on the other hand its valuation is off the charts. It appears that investors are not blind to this company's greatness, and they have already been bidding it up for the last year or so. This seems to be the case with a lot of companies these days. Investors and traders bid up most companies to such valuations that there is very little room for error and the margin of safety is very thin. This also makes it very difficult for new investors to find value plays which creates a loop. If potential investors can't find any good value plays and still want to be invested, they start ignoring valuations. This, in return, makes it more and more difficult to find value plays, and more and more investors are lured into ignoring valuations because they observe that they don't seem to matter as past investors before they made a lot of money by ignoring valuations, but how sustainable is this really? If you already own this stock I would keep holding but if you haven't already bought, I'd wait for more reasonable valuations.

data.title: Apple's AI Pivot Changes Everything (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Until now, investors had viewed Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) as a haven of stable cash flow, crafted from secure earnings and incremental, risk-free innovation. But in today's rapidly evolving world of high tech, that old narrative is no longer whole. It is time to look more carefully at Apple as doing more than holding its profitability machine, but subtly redefining its DNA to stay ahead of the curve for the artificial-intelligence era.

Macroeconomic headwinds, record-high quarterly earnings per share, and habitual product cycle upgrades notwithstanding, Apple’s slow-growth track record, high relative valuation multiples to its peers, and tariff exposures all combine to create an asymmetric investment narrative that’s less black-and-white than the “safe haven” tale would suggest.

With revenue in Q2 of FY25 of $95.4 billion, an increment of 5% from the previous year, and net earnings rising 4.8% to $24.8 billion, Apple is showing its operational prowess. But beneath these beats to headlines are cracks in its structural moat, cracks that its AI narrative, transformed supply chain, and activist buyback policy work to fill before these become larger valuation risks.

Apple’s Q3: Solid Numbers Are Likely, But the Real Signal Lies in the Subtext

Consensus is forecasting revenue of $88.8 billion and EPS of $1.42, just 1.78% year-over-year growth. Business as usual, on paper, the services revenue keeps growing, hardware is steady, and Apple, as ever, by a thread, exceeds expectations.

However, below that glossy surface, there are more subtle developments worth consideration. The strength of mainland China, supported by price actions and region-specific promos, could stealthily offset soft hardware globally. While services, that is, subscription and App Store growth, fatten margins, making the EPS line appear sturdier than initially assumed.

Here’s where things get even more interesting: the market may already have the steady execution of Apple built into its calculations, so upside surprises would have less to do with numbers and more to do with narrative. Investors would have less to do with Apple attaining $1.42 in EPS and more to do with what is being stated regarding rollout of AI. If initial traction for Apple Intelligence shows actual user adoption, the opposite of buzz, the Street could begin to modify its long-range valuation model.

Threatened tariffs for key supply chains, and an unusually high number of downward estimate cuts, however, indicate risk. If Apple blunts forward guidance or waves caution due to trade policy or AI ramp-up cost, the stock could mark time even on the headline beat. In short, Q2 might look predictable on the surface, but the subtext could stealthily redefine Apple’s near-term narrative.

Innovation or Insulation? Looking More Closely at the Main Model

Apple’s business model continues to revolve around an unprecedented hardware moat that’s topped off with a high-margin Services layer. The latest results have Products revenue coming in at $68.7 billion, expanding a modest 3% YoY, while Services grew 12% to an all-time high of $26.6 billion, in evidence of management’s attempts to look past hardware cycles.

Notably enough, the iPhone still accounts for nearly half of all sales, rising 2% to $46.8 billion. Behind this steady headline, however, iPhone upgrade volumes grew in double digits, while unit ASPs showed signs of promotional activity and tariff protection, a soft reminder that stickiness in this ecosystem now depends increasingly on value-for-money perception.

This balancing act is clearly visible in Apple’s shipment dynamics. As of Q1 2025, iPhone’s global market share slipped to 19%, down from a holiday-driven 23% peak just a quarter prior. While Q2 2025 results show revenue resilience, the trailing shipment trend hints at a tougher backdrop for unit economics and global demand momentum.

Segments of Mac and iPad had spectacular recoveries, as revenue of Mac rose 7% to $7.9 billion and that of iPad rose 15% to $6.4 billion, led by chips of M-series and boosts to performance. Segment of Wearables, Home, and Accessories declined 5% to $7.5 billion due to cycles of discretionary spending and declining novelty of Watch Series and AirPods. The out-of-whack segment profile makes sense for Apple to double down on the flywheel of Services: gross margin of Services still looks terrific, 75.7%, but for Products, for the quarter, 35.9%.

The subtle genius, and risk, of Apple’s approach is its incremental slide into an annuity-like basis of subs. Over 1 billion reported paid subs now, repeat revenue is evening out cyclic hardware blips. Apple, however, is so tied to App Store policy, never mind serial antitrust lawsuits and Europe’s Digital Markets Act that looks set to put that high-margin stream to the test, that the latest earnings call described alternative stores for app subs as endemic to today’s results but 2025-2026’s wild card.

Competitive Moats Under Pressure: Tariffs, Supply Chain Renegotiation, and an Arms Race in AI

Apple's weakest link which is the global supply chain, has become a double-edged tool. Q2 proof of demand verification verified an overweight percentage of iPhones used in the U.S. now emanating out of India, as Vietnam is responsible for virtually all Macs, iPads, and AirPods for the United States market.

Such diversification attempts to compensate for an anticipated tariff drag of $900 million in Q3 FY25 should tensions on world commerce persist. The kicker is Apple’s CFO famously announcing that build-head inventory plans have blunted short-term tariff impact but won’t insulate it for all eternity. Given the Section 232 chip probe still being conducted, potential downstream chip tariffs remain a threat that would force Apple either to absorb higher costs or pass them along to the consumer, crowding segment margins already compressed by ASP.

Meanwhile, the AI narrative is being positioned to arrive preceded by an exit from hardware. Tim Cook’s emphasis on Apple Intelligence and in-device LLMs is fascinating; the motivation to locally process large-bag LLMs within the A18 chips and the M-series Macs give away the hand to the future where Apple creates its user privacy moat and remains an insider to the generative AI explosion.

Apple has an issue here as even as its R&D spend is rising (up 8% year-to-date year to $8.55 billion for the quarter), its capital outlays on base models and on data centers have not yet achieved hyperscale competition. This model of in-house model + selective (ChatGPT) integration makes sense, but still not transformational enough to command valuation premia like that of dedicated AI platform plays.

Competition for high-end users also remains. Apple contained its Greater China sales decline to 2%, or flat FX-neutral, as a result of Chinese subsidies, but the region remains susceptible to home-bred competition as well as nationalist sentiment. Flat 1.73%–6.44% forecasted YoY EPS range for quarters ahead refers to that challenge. Even as it achieves high-margin expansion of business lines for its Services segment, hardware’s marginal advantages ever more struggle to uphold.

Financial Strength and Valuation Expansion: Is There Room for Multiple Expansion?

Apple's balance sheet shows total liabilities of $264.4 billion against shareholder equity of $66.8 billion, illustrating its net cash position that still underlies buybacks but also shows the magnitude of leverage engaged. Future buyback authorization of an additional $100 billion seeks to mitigate dilution and seasonal EPS expansion but is by itself not an expansionary impetus.

Valuation these days is pretty much the problem. With ~30x P/E for FY25 and a forward 2026 P/E of ~26.7x, Apple’s sector premium makes it practically near-term overvalued. Its forward 2.7x PEG ratio (non-GAAP FWD) is 46% above the sector median, so that’s too great of a price paid for earnings growth that’s going to remain in the low-to-mid single digits. Even by EV/Sales at 7.7x, that blows past its sector’s 3.5x, a high 124% premium that is going to have to get repriced rightly through continued leadership on the margin front and exemplary buyback execution.

Bottom Line for Investors

Apple continues to be an operational excellence and brand longevity masterclass, yet its high-valuation premium now depends so much on a capital return engine and a yet unproven AI bet. Investors should view this as a high-grade compounder, but one for which its multiple now reacts to even a slight decline in growth or geopolitical weakness. Long-term holders might trim off strength while keeping a close eye on AI implementation and tariff fallout. A new acquisition would do well to wait for the compression of the multiple or sturdier proof of monetization of the AI bet.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Mining Companies Lead The Calendar As More Sizable IPOs Submit Initial Filings
data.description:
Six issuers debuted in the US this past week, led by two mining companies. Three blank check companies also went public, and a handful of notable IPO candidates joined the pipeline.

Latin American miner Aura Minerals (AUGO) plans to raise $210 million at a $2.1 billion market cap. The company operates four wholly-owned operating mines in various countries in Latin America, as well as one mine in ramp-up phase. It primarily generates revenue from gold and, to a lesser extent, copper, with operations roughly evenly split between Brazil, Mexico, and Honduras. Aura Minerals finished down 1%.

Canada-listed Almonty Industries (ALM) plans to raise $75 million at a roughly $1.5 billion market cap. The company produces tungsten concentrate, primarily for the defense industry, with plans to vertically integrate into oxide production. Its flagship asset, the Sangdong Tungsten Mine in South Korea, is one of the world's largest tungsten deposits by Inferred Mineral Resource and is in soft commissioning. Almonty finished up 1%.

The week’s remaining deals came from small Asian issuers, led by Chinese supply chain management firm Majestic Ideal Holdings (MJID), which raised $15 million and finished down 45%.

Week Ahead

Four sizable IPOs are currently scheduled to list in the week ahead. Some small names may also join the calendar throughout the week.

Consumer intelligence provider NIQ Global Intelligence (NIQ) plans to raise $1.1 billion at a $6.5 billion market cap. The company is a leading global consumer intelligence company, and its global reach spans over 90 countries covering approximately 85% of the world's population, more than half the world's GDP, and more than $7.2 trillion in global consumer spend as of December 31, 2024. It has grown its revenue in recent years, aided by strong retention rates, although it remains loss-making, with a negative free cash flow.

Specialty insurance marketplace Accelerant (ARX) plans to raise $550 million at a $4.2 billion market cap. Accelerant operates a data-driven risk exchange that connects selected specialty insurance underwriters (sellers) with risk capital partners (buyers). On the "supply side" of its Risk Exchange, the company delivers a full service offering to members, while on the "demand side," the company offers risk capital partners a portfolio of specialty insurance premium that may otherwise be difficult to access elsewhere. As of March 31, 2025, Accelerant had 232 members and 96 risk capital partners on its platform and has grown Exchange Written Premium at a 217% CAGR since inception.

Leading educational publisher McGraw Hill (MH) plans to raise $500 million at a $4.0 billion market cap. McGraw Hill is a leading global provider of information solutions for education across K-12 to higher education and through professional learning. On an annual basis, the company serves approximately 60 million learners and educators. McGraw Hill is a top two provider in the K-12 market in the US, serving approximately 99% of public K-12 districts. It is also a top two provider of digital and print learning solutions in the US higher education market, serving approximately 82% of US higher education institutions, although it is highly leveraged at 3.8x net debt/EBITDA.

Spine surgery-focused medtech Carlsmed (CARL) plans to raise $101 million at a $430 million market cap. Its aprevo Technology Platform consists of AI-enabled software solutions, interbody implants custom designed for each patient's unique pathology and vertebral bone topography, and single-use surgical instruments. The company currently markets the aprevo Technology Platform for lumbar spine fusion surgery, and it is further developing the Platform for use in cervical spine fusion surgeries, which it expects to commercialize in 2026. As of March 31, 2025, 177 surgeon users had completed one or more procedures using the aprevo Technology Platform (+72% y/y).
